entities.name: vāyu
entities.cite: The breath, having come to rest upon the principle of the inner organ

entities.name: antaḥkaraṇa-tattva
entities.cite: the principle of the inner organ

entities.name: tejas
entities.cite: by the agency of the fire itself

axioms.name: asraya
axioms.cite: it has gone asrayatam, taken the antahkarana-tattva as its resort

axioms.name: samavista
axioms.cite: tad-dharmena samavista — suffused, occupied, by the inner organ's properties

axioms.name: vivartate
axioms.cite: the vivarta verb now applied to the physiological process itself

denials.name: parinamate
denials.cite: the vivarta verb tells against mere element-transformation

claims.name: asraya
claims.cite: having come to rest upon the principle of the inner organ

claims.name: vivartate
claims.cite: appears — by the agency of the fire itself

rejected_readings.label: physical_breath_mechanics
rejected_readings.rendering: Ordinary breath, having become associated with mental consciousness, gains the qualities of the mind and appears as visible wind.
rejected_readings.why: Treats vayu as mere physical substance (manifestation sort) rather than a subtle power. The sort error makes this reading incompatible with the axioms, which require vayu as power.

rejected_readings.label: element_transmutation
rejected_readings.rendering: The breath transforms into the luminous/visual element through the agency of the fire.
rejected_readings.why: Asserts parinamate (real transformation) of vayu into tejas, which the commentary explicitly rejects: vivarta indicates appearance without loss of identity, not elemental transmutation.

rejected_readings.label: reversed_dependency
rejected_readings.rendering: The inner organ, having come to rest upon the breath as its base, is pervaded by the breath's qualities.
rejected_readings.why: Reverses the asraya relation: it is the breath that takes the inner organ as its resort, not the inverse. The reversed relation is not entailed by the axioms.

notes: The verse explains how shaped breath becomes meaningful utterance by formalizing the middle joint of the speech-production cascade. The breath (vāyu), a subtle power, takes the inner organ (antaḥkaraṇa-tattva) as its substratum, becomes pervaded by its properties (dharma), and thereby manifests (vivartate) through the ripening fire (tejas)—revealing that physiological process is always already an appearance-of-mind rather than independent production. Avoids the element-physics reading by insisting vivarta (appearance without loss of identity) over parinama (transformation).
